title: Sort an array of strings by length
description: Sort strings based on their length

examples.explanation:
Initial: ["apple", "kiwi", "banana"]
Pass 1:
  Compare "apple"(5) and "kiwi"(4): swap → ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
  Compare "apple"(5) and "banana"(6): no swap → ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
  Swaps occurred, continue
Pass 2:
  Compare "kiwi"(4) and "apple"(5): no swap → ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
  Compare "apple"(5) and "banana"(6): no swap → ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
  No swaps, array sorted
Final: ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]

Initial: ["cat", "elephant", "dog", "ant"]
Pass 1:
  Compare "cat"(3) and "elephant"(8): swap → ["elephant", "cat", "dog", "ant"]
  Compare "cat"(3) and "dog"(3): no swap → ["elephant", "cat", "dog", "ant"]
  Compare "dog"(3) and "ant"(3): no swap → ["elephant", "cat", "dog", "ant"]
  Swaps occurred, continue
Pass 2:
  Compare "elephant"(8) and "cat"(3): swap → ["cat", "elephant", "dog", "ant"]
  Compare "elephant"(8) and "dog"(3): swap → ["cat", "dog", "elephant", "ant"]
  Compare "elephant"(8) and "ant"(3): swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]
  Swaps occurred, continue
Pass 3:
  Compare "cat"(3) and "dog"(3): no swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]
  Compare "dog"(3) and "ant"(3): no swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]
  Compare "ant"(3) and "elephant"(8): no swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]
  No swaps, array sorted
Final: ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Bubble Sort O(n²)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly compare adjacent strings and swap them if they are in the wrong order based on length, continuing until no swaps are needed. This is a simple but inefficient method suitable for small datasets or educational purposes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Strings by Length Using Bubble Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of bubble sort to sort an array of strings by their length in ascending order.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the brute force bubble sort approach. The intuition is that by repeatedly comparing adjacent strings and swapping them if they're in the wrong order (based on length), we can gradually 'bubble' longer strings to the end of the array. This works because each full pass through the array ensures at least one element is placed in its correct final position. The algorithm starts by assuming the array isn't sorted, then repeatedly scans through the array comparing adjacent elements. If a longer string comes before a shorter one, we swap them. We continue until a complete pass occurs with no swaps, indicating the array is sorted. The time complexity is O(n²) because in the worst case (reverse sorted array), we need n-1 passes, each comparing n-1 pairs. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra space for swapping. This connects to L1 by using the swap operation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to track whether swaps occurred (leading to unnecessary passes), incorrect loop boundaries (off-by-one errors), and not handling empty strings or arrays properly. The real pattern involves nested loops with a swap flag.

examples.trace:
Initial: ["apple", "kiwi", "banana"]
Pass 1:
  Compare "apple"(5) and "kiwi"(4): swap → ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
  Compare "apple"(5) and "banana"(6): no swap → ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
  Swaps occurred, continue
Pass 2:
  Compare "kiwi"(4) and "apple"(5): no swap → ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
  Compare "apple"(5) and "banana"(6): no swap → ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
  No swaps, array sorted
Final: ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]

Initial: ["cat", "elephant", "dog", "ant"]
Pass 1:
  Compare "cat"(3) and "elephant"(8): swap → ["elephant", "cat", "dog", "ant"]
  Compare "cat"(3) and "dog"(3): no swap → ["elephant", "cat", "dog", "ant"]
  Compare "dog"(3) and "ant"(3): no swap → ["elephant", "cat", "dog", "ant"]
  Swaps occurred, continue
Pass 2:
  Compare "elephant"(8) and "cat"(3): swap → ["cat", "elephant", "dog", "ant"]
  Compare "elephant"(8) and "dog"(3): swap → ["cat", "dog", "elephant", "ant"]
  Compare "elephant"(8) and "ant"(3): swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]
  Swaps occurred, continue
Pass 3:
  Compare "cat"(3) and "dog"(3): no swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]
  Compare "dog"(3) and "ant"(3): no swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]
  Compare "ant"(3) and "elephant"(8): no swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]
  No swaps, array sorted
Final: ["cat", "dog", "ant", "elephant"]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare and Swap Adjacent Strings by Length
ladders.desc: Implement a function that compares two adjacent strings and swaps them if they're in the wrong length order.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function in bubble sort. The intuition is that sorting requires comparing elements and rearranging them when they're out of order. For string length sorting, we need to compare the lengths of two strings and swap their positions if the first is longer than the second. This creates the basic 'bubbling' action where longer strings move toward the end. The algorithm takes an array and an index i, compares the lengths of elements at positions i and i+1, and swaps them if length[i] > length[i+1]. We return a boolean indicating whether a swap occurred. Time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison and potential swap. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use temporary variables. This connects to L0 by being the core operation repeated in the bubble sort passes. Common mistakes include comparing strings lexicographically instead of by length, forgetting to return the swap status, and incorrect array bounds. The real pattern involves a simple if-check with a length comparison and three-line swap.

examples.trace:
Compare "apple" length 5 and "kiwi" length 4
5 > 4, so swap positions 0 and 1
Array becomes ["kiwi", "apple", "banana"]
Return true because swap occurred

Compare "dog" length 3 and "elephant" length 8
3 > 8 is false, so no swap needed
Array remains ["cat", "dog", "elephant"]
Return false because no swap occurred

ladders.title: Core Logic — Single Pass Through Array with Adjacent Comparisons
ladders.desc: Perform one complete pass through the array, comparing and potentially swapping each adjacent pair.
ladders.explanation: This represents the central technique of bubble sort: making a single pass through the array to partially sort it. The intuition is that by comparing each element with its neighbor and swapping when needed, we ensure that after one pass, the largest element (longest string) 'bubbles up' to its correct position at the end. The algorithm iterates from the first element to the second-to-last, applying the compare-and-swap operation to each adjacent pair. We track whether any swaps occurred during the pass. Time complexity is O(n) for one pass through n-1 comparisons. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by repeatedly using the compare-and-swap subroutine, and to L0 by being the building block for the complete sorting algorithm (which repeats passes until sorted). Common mistakes include going out of array bounds (comparing last element with non-existent next), not tracking swap status correctly, and misunderstanding that one pass doesn't fully sort the array. The real pattern is a for-loop with a swap flag.

examples.trace:
Start: ["banana", "apple", "kiwi"], swap_flag = false
Compare "banana"(6) and "apple"(5): swap → ["apple", "banana", "kiwi"], flag = true
Compare "banana"(6) and "kiwi"(4): swap → ["apple", "kiwi", "banana"], flag = true
End of pass: array = ["apple", "kiwi", "banana"], swaps occurred = true

Start: ["cat", "dog", "ant"], swap_flag = false
Compare "cat"(3) and "dog"(3): no swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant"], flag = false
Compare "dog"(3) and "ant"(3): no swap → ["cat", "dog", "ant"], flag = false
End of pass: array = ["cat", "dog", "ant"], swaps occurred = false

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare String Lengths
ladders.desc: Compare the lengths of two strings and determine their ordering relationship.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed because bubble sort requires comparing elements. For string length sorting, we need to compare string lengths rather than the strings themselves. The intuition is that sorting requires a comparison function that defines 'less than' or 'greater than' relationships. Here, 'shorter' means 'less than' for ascending order. The algorithm simply takes two strings, computes their lengths using a length function, and returns whether the first is shorter than, equal to, or longer than the second. Time complexity is O(1) assuming length computation is constant time (true for most languages). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the comparison logic for the adjacent pairs, and to L4 which provides the length operation. Common mistakes include comparing the strings directly (lexicographic order), forgetting that equal lengths don't require swapping, and not understanding that we only need a binary 'greater than' check for bubble sort. The real pattern is comparing len(str1) and len(str2).

examples.trace:
Length of "apple" = 5
Length of "kiwi" = 4
5 > 4, so "apple" is longer than "kiwi"

Length of "cat" = 3
Length of "dog" = 3
3 == 3, so "cat" is equal in length to "dog"

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Get Length of a String
ladders.desc: Determine how many characters are in a given string.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is essential because sorting by length requires knowing how long each string is. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and their length is a fundamental property. The algorithm is simple: count the number of characters in the string. In programming, this is typically a built-in function or property. Time complexity is O(1) in most languages as length is stored as metadata. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by providing the length values needed for comparison, and to L5 which establishes what strings are. Common mistakes include counting bytes instead of characters (important for Unicode), forgetting that empty string has length 0, and not realizing that length is a property not a method in some languages. The real pattern is calling len(string) or string.length.

examples.trace:
String: h-e-l-l-o
Count characters: 1(h), 2(e), 3(l), 4(l), 5(o)
Total length = 5

Empty string has no characters
Count characters: 0
Total length = 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a String?
ladders.desc: Understand that a string is a sequence of characters with a measurable length.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what strings are. The intuition is that strings are fundamental data types representing text, composed of characters in sequence. A string has properties including its content (the characters) and its length (number of characters). The algorithm concept here is just the definition: a string = ordered collection of characters. Time/space analysis doesn't apply at this conceptual level. This connects to L4 by establishing that strings have lengths we can measure. Common mistakes include confusing strings with numbers, not understanding that spaces and punctuation are characters too, and thinking strings are fixed length. The real pattern is recognizing that "hello" is a string of 5 characters. This foundation is crucial because without understanding strings, we can't understand string operations like length measurement.

examples.output: String with 3 characters: c, a, t
examples.trace:
The string "cat" consists of:
Character 1: 'c'
Character 2: 'a'
Character 3: 't'
Total: 3 characters in sequence

The empty string has no characters
It's still a valid string, just with length 0

testCases.expected: 5 characters: h, e, l, l, o
5 characters: a, b, space, c, d

approaches.name: Approach 2: Comparison-Based Sort O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort with a custom comparator that compares string lengths. This leverages efficient sorting libraries and is optimal for general-purpose sorting by length.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for mergesort or O(log n) for quicksort (average case)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Array of Strings by Length Using Comparison-Based Sort
ladders.desc: Complete solution implementing a custom comparator for string length sorting
ladders.explanation: This solution sorts an array of strings based on their character length using a comparison-based sorting algorithm with a custom comparator. The intuition is that standard sorting algorithms compare elements pairwise, and by modifying the comparison logic to focus on string lengths rather than lexicographic order, we can efficiently sort by length. We'll use a stable sorting algorithm (like mergesort) to maintain relative order of strings with equal lengths. The algorithm works by: 1) Taking an input array of strings, 2) Using a sorting function with a custom comparator that compares string lengths, 3) Returning the sorted array. The comparator function takes two strings a and b, compares len(a) and len(b), and returns -1 if len(a) < len(b), 0 if equal, and 1 if len(a) > len(b). This approach leverages efficient O(n log n) sorting algorithms while being easy to implement. Time complexity is O(n log n) for the sorting plus O(n) for length comparisons, which simplifies to O(n log n). Space complexity is O(n) for mergesort's auxiliary array or O(log n) for quicksort's recursion stack. Connection to L1: This full solution directly uses the custom comparator function from L1 within a standard sorting framework. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to handle empty strings, 2) Using unstable sort when maintaining original order for equal lengths matters, 3) Incorrect comparator return values, 4) Not considering Unicode characters in length calculation. Real code pattern: def sort_by_length(arr): return sorted(arr, key=lambda s: len(s)) or with custom comparator: def compare(a, b): return (len(a) > len(b)) - (len(a) < len(b)); arr.sort(key=cmp_to_key(compare))

examples.trace:
Input: ["apple", "kiwi", "banana", "pear"]
Step 1: Extract lengths: [5, 4, 6, 4]
Step 2: Sort using comparator:
- Compare "apple"(5) and "kiwi"(4): 5 > 4, so "kiwi" comes first
- Compare "banana"(6) and "pear"(4): 6 > 4, so "pear" comes first
- After sorting: ["kiwi", "pear", "apple", "banana"]
Step 3: For equal lengths ("kiwi" and "pear" both 4), stable sort maintains input order
Final: ["pear", "kiwi", "apple", "banana"] ("pear" before "kiwi" in original)

Input: ["", "a", "abc", "ab", "abcd"]
Step 1: Lengths: [0, 1, 3, 2, 4]
Step 2: Sort using comparator:
- All comparisons based on length values
- Empty string (0) is smallest
- "a" (1) < "ab" (2) < "abc" (3) < "abcd" (4)
Step 3: Stable sort maintains order for equal lengths (none here)
Final: ["", "a", "ab", "abc", "abcd"]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Custom Comparator Function for String Length Comparison
ladders.desc: Implement a comparator that compares two strings based on their lengths
ladders.explanation: The custom comparator function is the heart of the length-based sorting approach. Its purpose is to define how two strings should be ordered based on their character counts rather than alphabetical order. The intuition is that sorting algorithms need a way to compare elements, and by providing a function that returns whether one string is shorter, equal, or longer than another, we can sort by length. The algorithm: 1) Take two string inputs a and b, 2) Calculate len(a) and len(b), 3) Return -1 if len(a) < len(b) (a should come before b), 0 if len(a) == len(b) (order doesn't matter based on length), 1 if len(a) > len(b) (a should come after b). This follows the standard comparator convention used in many programming languages. Time complexity is O(1) since length calculation is constant time for strings (most languages store length). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This comparator function is passed to the sorting algorithm in the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the core comparison logic. Common mistakes: 1) Returning boolean instead of three-way comparison values, 2) Comparing strings lexicographically instead of by length, 3) Not handling empty strings correctly, 4) Forgetting that some languages use negative/zero/positive return convention. Real code pattern: def compare_by_length(a, b): la, lb = len(a), len(b); return -1 if la < lb else (1 if la > lb else 0)

examples.trace:
Input strings: "apple" (length 5) and "banana" (length 6)
Step 1: Calculate len("apple") = 5
Step 2: Calculate len("banana") = 6
Step 3: Compare 5 < 6 → true
Step 4: Return -1 (indicating "apple" should come before "banana")

Input strings: "cat" (length 3) and "dog" (length 3)
Step 1: Calculate len("cat") = 3
Step 2: Calculate len("dog") = 3
Step 3: Compare 3 == 3 → true
Step 4: Return 0 (indicating equal length, order doesn't matter based on length)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing String Lengths Instead of Lexicographic Order
ladders.desc: Understand that sorting by length requires comparing character counts, not alphabetical order
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that to sort strings by length, we must compare their character counts rather than their alphabetical (lexicographic) order. Standard string sorting compares characters position by position (e.g., "apple" vs "banana" compares 'a' vs 'b'), but for length sorting, we only care about the count of characters. The intuition is that length is a numerical property of strings, and we can extract this property (via len() function) to use as the sorting key. This represents a shift from comparing string content to comparing string metadata. The algorithm: 1) For any two strings, ignore their actual characters, 2) Focus only on their length values, 3) Establish ordering based on which length is smaller/larger. This works because sorting algorithms only need a consistent way to compare elements - whether we compare characters or lengths doesn't matter to the algorithm. Time complexity for the comparison operation is O(1) since length is typically stored. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This insight is implemented in the comparator function. Connection to L3: This builds upon understanding what string length means. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing length sorting with alphabetical sorting, 2) Thinking special characters or spaces affect length differently (they don't - all characters count), 3) Forgetting that Unicode characters may count as multiple bytes but typically as one character for length. Real code pattern: The key realization is that len(s) gives us a number we can compare instead of comparing s directly.

examples.input: Should "apple" come before "banana" when sorting by length?
examples.trace:
Step 1: Get lengths: len("apple") = 5, len("banana") = 6
Step 2: Compare numerically: 5 < 6
Step 3: Therefore "apple" (shorter) should come before "banana" (longer)
Note: Alphabetically, "apple" would come before "banana" anyway, but for different reasons

examples.input: Should "cat" come before "dog" when sorting by length?
examples.trace:
Step 1: Get lengths: len("cat") = 3, len("dog") = 3
Step 2: Compare numerically: 3 == 3
Step 3: Both have same length, so order doesn't matter based on length
A stable sort would maintain their original relative order

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculating String Length
ladders.desc: Determine the number of characters in a string
ladders.explanation: String length calculation is fundamental to length-based sorting. The intuition is that every string has a property called 'length' representing how many characters it contains, and we need to reliably extract this numerical value. In most programming languages, strings are stored as sequences of characters (or bytes for Unicode), and the length operation counts these elements. The algorithm: 1) Start at the beginning of the string, 2) Iterate through each character, 3) Increment a counter for each character encountered, 4) Return the final count. Alternatively, most languages provide a built-in len() or length() function that does this efficiently, often in O(1) time by storing the length as metadata. Understanding that spaces, punctuation, digits, and special characters all count as characters is crucial. Time complexity is O(n) if actually counting (where n is string length), but typically O(1) with built-in functions. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: Length values are what we compare for sorting. Connection to L4: This builds upon accessing individual string characters. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing string length with memory size (bytes), 2) Forgetting that newline or tab characters count, 3) Thinking empty string has length 1 (it's 0), 4) Not considering Unicode characters that may display as one but be multiple code points. Real code pattern: def string_length(s): count = 0; for char in s: count += 1; return count. Or simply len(s) in Python.

examples.trace:
String: h e l l o
Step 1: Initialize count = 0
Step 2: Process 'h' → count = 1
Step 3: Process 'e' → count = 2
Step 4: Process 'l' → count = 3
Step 5: Process 'l' → count = 4
Step 6: Process 'o' → count = 5
Final: Length = 5

String: (empty)
Step 1: Initialize count = 0
Step 2: No characters to process
Final: Length = 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Individual Characters in a String
ladders.desc: Retrieve specific characters from a string by position
ladders.explanation: Accessing individual characters is essential for understanding strings as sequences, which underpins length calculation. The intuition is that strings are ordered collections of characters, and we can reference each character by its position (index). In most languages, string indexing starts at 0 for the first character. The algorithm: 1) Have a string s, 2) Use index i (typically 0 ≤ i < length), 3) Retrieve character at position i using s[i] notation. This operation is typically O(1) time since strings allow random access. Understanding zero-based indexing is crucial: first character is s[0], second is s[1], etc. The last character is at index len(s)-1. Attempting to access beyond valid indices causes errors. Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation. Connection to L3: Character access is how we would manually count string length. Connection to L5: This builds on the concept of strings as data. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (thinking first character is s[1]), 2) Accessing index equal to length (should be length-1), 3) Not handling empty strings (no valid indices), 4) Confusing character with substring. Real code pattern: first_char = s[0]; last_char = s[len(s)-1]; iterate with for i in range(len(s)): char = s[i]

examples.trace:
String: "hello" (indices: 0:h, 1:e, 2:l, 3:l, 4:o)
Step 1: Identify index 0
Step 2: Retrieve character at position 0
Result: 'h' (the first character)

String: "world" (length 5, indices 0-4)
Step 1: Calculate length = 5, so valid indices are 0 through 4
Step 2: Index 4 is the last position (since 5-1 = 4)
Step 3: Retrieve character at position 4
Result: 'd' (the last character)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Sequences of Characters
ladders.desc: Recognize that strings are ordered collections of characters
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept establishes that strings are not atomic entities but rather sequences (ordered lists) of individual characters. The intuition is that just as arrays contain elements, strings contain characters in a specific order. Each character can be letters, digits, spaces, or symbols. The string "hello" consists of characters 'h', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o' in that exact order. This ordering matters - "hello" is different from "olleh" even though they contain the same characters. Strings have a beginning (first character) and end (last character). The length of a string is simply how many characters it contains. Empty string ("") contains zero characters. This concept is prerequisite because without understanding strings as character sequences, we cannot meaningfully talk about their length or access individual characters. Time/space analysis isn't applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. Connection to L4: This understanding enables character access operations. Common mistakes: 1) Treating strings as single units without internal structure, 2) Confusing strings with numbers ("123" is three characters, not the number 123), 3) Not realizing spaces are characters too, 4) Thinking string manipulation changes the original (often strings are immutable). Real code pattern: In Python, strings are defined with quotes: s = "example". They support operations like concatenation (+), repetition (*), and membership testing (in).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Identify the string: "cat"
Step 2: Break into individual characters:
- First character: 'c'
- Second character: 'a'
- Third character: 't'
Step 3: Verify order: c then a then t
Result: Sequence of three characters: 'c', 'a', 't'

Step 1: Analyze "ab": characters 'a' then 'b'
Step 2: Analyze "ba": characters 'b' then 'a'
Step 3: Compare:
- Both contain same characters: 'a' and 'b'
- But order differs: "ab" has a first, "ba" has b first
Step 4: Conclusion: Different sequences, hence different strings

testCases.input: String containing letters A, B, C in order?

approaches.name: Approach 3: Counting Sort by Length O(n + k)
approaches.summary: Count the frequency of each string length, then build a sorted array by placing strings into buckets based on their lengths. This is efficient when the range of lengths (k) is small compared to the number of strings (n).
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n + k) where k is the range of lengths

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Strings by Length Using Counting Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of counting sort approach to sort strings by their length.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages counting sort to efficiently sort strings by length when the range of lengths (k) is small compared to the number of strings (n). The intuition is that instead of comparing strings directly, we can count how many strings have each length, then use these counts to determine where each string should go in the sorted output. This works because string length is an integer key with limited range. The algorithm proceeds in three phases: First, find the minimum and maximum string lengths to determine the range k. Second, count the frequency of each length. Third, compute prefix sums to determine starting positions for each length in the output array. Finally, place each string in its correct position in a stable manner. Time complexity is O(n + k) where n is number of strings and k is range of lengths. Space complexity is O(n + k) for the count array and output array. This approach is optimal when k is small, avoiding O(n log n) comparison-based sorting. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty strings (length 0), not maintaining stability (which isn't required for this problem but good practice), and off-by-one errors when computing indices. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the prefix sum computation with the placement logic.

examples.trace:
Input: ["apple", "kiwi", "banana", "pear"]
1. Find min_len = 4 ("kiwi", "pear"), max_len = 6 ("banana"), range k = 3 (4,5,6)
2. Count frequencies: length 4: 2 ("kiwi", "pear"), length 5: 1 ("apple"), length 6: 1 ("banana") → count = [2,1,1]
3. Compute prefix sums: start positions: length 4 at index 0, length 5 at 2, length 6 at 3 → prefix = [0,2,3]
4. Place strings: Initialize output = [None]*4
   - "apple" (len 5): pos = prefix[1] = 2, output[2] = "apple", increment prefix[1] to 3
   - "kiwi" (len 4): pos = prefix[0] = 0, output[0] = "kiwi", increment prefix[0] to 1
   - "banana" (len 6): pos = prefix[2] = 3, output[3] = "banana", increment prefix[2] to 4
   - "pear" (len 4): pos = prefix[0] = 1, output[1] = "pear", increment prefix[0] to 2
5. Output: ["kiwi", "pear", "apple", "banana"] sorted by length (4,4,5,6)

Input: ["", "a", "bb", "ccc", "dddd"]
1. Find min_len = 0 (""), max_len = 4 ("dddd"), range k = 5 (0,1,2,3,4)
2. Count frequencies: length 0:1, 1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 → count = [1,1,1,1,1]
3. Compute prefix sums: start positions: length 0 at 0, 1 at 1, 2 at 2, 3 at 3, 4 at 4 → prefix = [0,1,2,3,4]
4. Place strings: Initialize output = [None]*5
   - "" (len 0): pos = prefix[0] = 0, output[0] = "", increment prefix[0] to 1
   - "a" (len 1): pos = prefix[1] = 1, output[1] = "a", increment prefix[1] to 2
   - "bb" (len 2): pos = prefix[2] = 2, output[2] = "bb", increment prefix[2] to 3
   - "ccc" (len 3): pos = prefix[3] = 3, output[3] = "ccc", increment prefix[3] to 4
   - "dddd" (len 4): pos = prefix[4] = 4, output[4] = "dddd", increment prefix[4] to 5
5. Output: ["", "a", "bb", "ccc", "dddd"] already sorted by length (0,1,2,3,4)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Prefix Sums for Placement Indices
ladders.desc: Transform frequency counts into starting positions for each length in the output array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of counting sort. After counting how many strings have each length, we need to determine where each length group should start in the final sorted array. The intuition is that if we know there are C strings of length L, and we know where strings of shorter lengths end, we can calculate the starting position for length L. This is done by computing prefix sums (cumulative sums) of the frequency array. The algorithm: Given an array count where count[i] = number of strings with length i (relative to min_len), initialize prefix array of same size. Set prefix[0] = 0 (strings of minimum length start at position 0). For i from 1 to len(count)-1, prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + count[i-1]. This means prefix[i] gives the starting index in the output array for strings of length (min_len + i). Time complexity is O(k) where k is range of lengths. Space complexity is O(k) for the prefix array. This feeds into L0 by providing the indices needed to place strings correctly. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization (should be 0, not count[0]), off-by-one errors in the loop, and misunderstanding that prefix[i] points to where the next string of that length should go. The real code pattern: prefix = [0]*len(count); for i in range(1, len(count)): prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + count[i-1].

examples.trace:
Input count = [2,3,1]
1. Initialize prefix = [0,0,0]
2. prefix[0] = 0 (by definition)
3. i=1: prefix[1] = prefix[0] + count[0] = 0 + 2 = 2
4. i=2: prefix[2] = prefix[1] + count[1] = 2 + 3 = 5
5. Result prefix = [0,2,5]
Interpretation: Strings of length 4 start at index 0, length 5 at index 2, length 6 at index 5 in output array.

Input count = [1,0,2,0,1]
1. Initialize prefix = [0,0,0,0,0]
2. prefix[0] = 0
3. i=1: prefix[1] = 0 + 1 = 1
4. i=2: prefix[2] = 1 + 0 = 1
5. i=3: prefix[3] = 1 + 2 = 3
6. i=4: prefix[4] = 3 + 0 = 3
7. Result prefix = [0,1,1,3,3]
Interpretation: Length 0 at index 0, length 1 at 1, length 2 at 1, length 3 at 3, length 4 at 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Count Frequencies of String Lengths
ladders.desc: Create a frequency array counting how many strings have each possible length.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental counting step. The intuition is that we're converting the sorting problem into a counting problem: instead of comparing strings, we simply tally how many strings have each length. This works because the sort key (length) is an integer with bounded range. The algorithm: First, find min_len and max_len by iterating through all strings. Then create an array count of size (max_len - min_len + 1), initialized to zeros. Iterate through strings again, for each string compute its length L, increment count[L - min_len] by 1. This gives us a histogram of lengths. Time complexity is O(n + k) where n is number of strings (for two passes) and k is range of lengths (for array initialization). Space complexity is O(k) for the count array. This feeds into L1 by providing the frequency data needed for prefix sum computation. Common mistakes include not handling empty strings (length 0), incorrect array size calculation, or forgetting to offset by min_len when indexing. The real code pattern: min_len = min(len(s) for s in strings); max_len = max(len(s) for s in strings); count = [0]*(max_len-min_len+1); for s in strings: count[len(s)-min_len] += 1.

examples.trace:
Input strings: ["hi","hello","hey","hi"]
1. Find lengths: [2,5,3,2]
2. min_len = 2, max_len = 5
3. Range size = 5-2+1 = 4 → count = [0,0,0,0]
4. Process each string:
   - "hi" length 2: index 2-2=0, count[0] becomes 1
   - "hello" length 5: index 5-2=3, count[3] becomes 1
   - "hey" length 3: index 3-2=1, count[1] becomes 1
   - "hi" length 2: index 0, count[0] becomes 2
5. Final count = [2,1,0,1]
Interpretation: 2 strings of length 2, 1 of length 3, 0 of length 4, 1 of length 5.

Input strings: ["","a","","bb"]
1. Find lengths: [0,1,0,2]
2. min_len = 0, max_len = 2
3. Range size = 2-0+1 = 3 → count = [0,0,0]
4. Process each string:
   - "" length 0: index 0-0=0, count[0] becomes 1
   - "a" length 1: index 1-0=1, count[1] becomes 1
   - "" length 0: index 0, count[0] becomes 2
   - "bb" length 2: index 2-0=2, count[2] becomes 1
5. Final count = [2,1,1]
Interpretation: 2 empty strings, 1 length 1, 1 length 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Minimum and Maximum Values in an Array
ladders.desc: Determine the smallest and largest string lengths in the array.
ladders.explanation: This is a prerequisite for counting sort because we need to know the range of keys (lengths) to create our count array. The intuition is simple: we scan through all elements once, keeping track of the smallest and largest values seen so far. For strings, we're interested in their lengths, not the strings themselves. The algorithm: Initialize min_val to infinity (or a very large number) and max_val to negative infinity (or a very small number). Iterate through each string in the array. For each string, compute its length L. If L < min_val, update min_val = L. If L > max_val, update max_val = L. After processing all strings, we have the range [min_val, max_val]. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of strings. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the bounds needed to create the frequency array. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization (e.g., using 0 for min_val when lengths could be 0), not handling empty arrays, or confusing string length with string value. The real code pattern: min_len = float('inf'); max_len = float('-inf'); for s in strings: L = len(s); if L < min_len: min_len = L; if L > max_len: max_len = L.

examples.trace:
Input strings: ["apple","kiwi","banana"]
1. Initialize min_len = inf, max_len = -inf
2. Process "apple": length=5, min_len=5, max_len=5
3. Process "kiwi": length=4, 4<5 so min_len=4, max_len stays 5
4. Process "banana": length=6, 6>5 so max_len=6, min_len stays 4
5. Final: min_len=4, max_len=6

Input strings: ["","hello","world"]
1. Initialize min_len = inf, max_len = -inf
2. Process "": length=0, min_len=0, max_len=0
3. Process "hello": length=5, 5>0 so max_len=5, min_len stays 0
4. Process "world": length=5, no change to min or max
5. Final: min_len=0, max_len=5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Get Length of a String
ladders.desc: Compute the number of characters in a string.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation needed throughout the algorithm. The intuition is that we need to extract the sort key (length) from each string. In most programming languages, strings have a built-in length property or function that returns the number of characters. For counting sort, we treat this length as an integer key. The algorithm is trivial: given a string s, return len(s). Time complexity is O(1) in most languages (since length is often stored). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the values we compare when finding min/max. Common mistakes include counting bytes instead of characters (in some encodings), forgetting that empty string has length 0, or trying to use string comparison instead of length comparison. The real code pattern: length = len(string).

examples.trace: String "hello" has characters: h,e,l,l,o → count = 5
Empty string has no characters → count = 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a String?
ladders.desc: Understand strings as sequences of characters with measurable length.
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute foundation. A string is a data type that represents text as a sequence of characters. Characters can be letters, digits, symbols, or spaces. The length of a string is simply the number of characters it contains. For example, "cat" has 3 characters (c, a, t), so its length is 3. An empty string "" has 0 characters. The intuition for sorting: when we sort strings by length, we're ordering them based on this count, not alphabetically. This concept feeds into L4 because we need to understand what we're measuring. Common beginner mistakes include confusing string length with string value (e.g., thinking "10" is longer than "2" because 10>2, but actually both have length 2 and 1 respectively), or not realizing spaces count as characters. In programming, strings are typically immutable and have operations like len() to get length.

examples.trace: String "apple" consists of characters: position 1: 'a', 2: 'p', 3: 'p', 4: 'l', 5: 'e'. Total count = 5.
Empty string "" has no characters. Its length is 0.
